questions.question: A $60\text{ kg}$ ice skater at rest pushes a $3\text{ kg}$ ball forward at $10\text{ m/s}$. What is the skater's velocity after the throw?
questions.explanation: $0 = (60 \times v) + (3 \times 10)$. $60v = -30$. $v = -0.5\text{ m/s}$ — the skater recoils backward at $0.5\text{ m/s}$.

questions.question: A stationary cannon fires a $4\text{ kg}$ cannonball forward at $150\text{ m/s}$. The cannon recoils backward at $2\text{ m/s}$. What is the mass of the cannon?
A $10\text{ kg}$ bomb at rest explodes into two pieces. A $4\text{ kg}$ piece flies east at $15\text{ m/s}$. What is the velocity of the $6\text{ kg}$ piece?
A $9\text{ kg}$ object at rest explodes into a $3\text{ kg}$ piece and a $6\text{ kg}$ piece. The $3\text{ kg}$ piece moves at $12\text{ m/s}$. What is the total kinetic energy after the explosion?

A stationary object explodes into two pieces. One piece has mass $2\text{ kg}$ and moves at $15\text{ m/s}$. The total kinetic energy after the explosion is $300\text{ J}$. What is the mass of the second piece?
A $3\text{ kg}$ rifle fires a $0.02\text{ kg}$ bullet at $900\text{ m/s}$ forward. What is the recoil velocity of the rifle?
An $80\text{ kg}$ astronaut at rest throws a $5\text{ kg}$ tool forward at $8\text{ m/s}$. What happens to the astronaut?
options: The astronaut moves forward at $0.5\text{ m/s}$
The astronaut moves backward at $0.5\text{ m/s}$
The astronaut moves backward at $2\text{ m/s}$

questions.question: A $15\text{ kg}$ object at rest explodes into a $6\text{ kg}$ piece and a $9\text{ kg}$ piece. The $9\text{ kg}$ piece moves south at $8\text{ m/s}$. What is the velocity of the $6\text{ kg}$ piece?
A $50\text{ kg}$ skater at rest throws a $2\text{ kg}$ ball forward at $25\text{ m/s}$. What is the total kinetic energy after the throw?

A stationary $20\text{ kg}$ bomb explodes into three pieces. Piece A ($5\text{ kg}$) moves east at $12\text{ m/s}$. Piece B ($7\text{ kg}$) moves west at $4\text{ m/s}$. What is the velocity of Piece C ($8\text{ kg}$)?
A stationary object explodes into two pieces. Piece X moves twice as fast as Piece Y. What is the ratio of their masses?
questions.explanation: $m_X \times 2v_Y = -m_Y \times v_Y$. $2m_X = m_Y$. So $m_X : m_Y = 1 : 2$ — the faster piece has half the mass.

questions.question: A $6\text{ kg}$ object at rest explodes into two equal $3\text{ kg}$ pieces. They move apart at $8\text{ m/s}$ each in opposite directions. What fraction of the total kinetic energy does each piece carry?
questions.explanation: $KE_{each} = \frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 8^2 = 96\text{ J}$. Total $KE = 192\text{ J}$. Each carries $96/192 = \frac{1}{2}$ of the total.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered explosions, recoil, and momentum conservation!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review three-piece explosions and mass-velocity ratios.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on $0 = m_1v_1 + m_2v_2$ and solving for the unknown variable.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: in explosions, total momentum before is zero, and pieces move in opposite directions.
